title: Cathead biscuits
subtitle: Southern drop biscuits, no rolling required
excerpt: Large, rough-edged Southern American drop biscuits made with cold butter and cold buttermilk. The dough drops straight from the spoon onto the tray with no rolling and no cutters. Makes ten.
sourceNotes: Lettice Bryan, The Kentucky Housewife (1839), public domain US cookbook: drop biscuits are recorded made with flour, lard, and soured milk, dropped from a spoon without rolling. USDA Cooperative Extension Service publications on Southern baking traditions document the cathead style (large, rough-shaped, roughly the span of a cat's head) as the defining regional variation from the rolled-and-cut biscuit.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled biscuits in a bag for up to 2 months. Reheat from frozen at 180°C for 8 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Double and bake on two trays at once.

content.text: Cathead biscuits are named for their size: roughly the span of a cat's head. The dough is too soft to roll, which makes them faster than almost any other biscuit. Cold butter, cold buttermilk, and a light hand at mixing are the three requirements. The rough edges that come from dropping the dough straight onto the tray are the point, not a flaw.

Preheat the oven to 230°C fan. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Whisk the {{self-raising-flour}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} together in a large bowl. Rub the {{unsalted-butter}} into the flour with your fingertips until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs with some pea-sized butter pieces still visible; those pieces are what produce the flaky pull when the biscuits bake. Add the {{buttermilk}} and stir briefly with a fork until a shaggy, sticky dough forms. Stop as soon as there are no dry streaks; overworking makes the biscuits tough. Using two spoons or a large ice-cream scoop, drop rough mounds of about 80 g each onto the lined tray, spacing them 5 cm apart. Bake for 12–14 minutes until the tops are deep gold and the bases sound hollow when tapped. Transfer to a rack and serve warm.
